title: Nothing works with my kid

presenting_issue.label: Nothing works with my kid
presenting_issue.description: I've tried everything with my kid. Consequences, rewards, talking it through. Nothing works. Everything the books say just makes it worse.

core_issue.label: Conventional parenting doesn't work and alternatives are unknown
core_issue.description:
Parenting a neurodivergent child using neurotypical strategies that keep failing. Punishment doesn't work. Reward charts don't work. The parenting books don't apply. They're trying harder at the wrong things while the child struggles and they feel increasingly like failures. Need a completely different framework but don't know it exists.
Awareness: Low. Thinks they need to try harder at conventional approaches rather than needing a different paradigm.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Mental health provider gave up
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Their teen's therapist terminated treatment because the child wouldn't engage. They're back to square one with a longer waitlist.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Physical reaction to child's yelling
core_adjacent_attributes.content: When their child raises their voice, their heart starts pounding and their hands tremble before they even consciously register what's happening.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Unfavorably compares self to other parents
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Watches other parents at school pickup and assumes they have it more together, are more patient, have happier children.

modifiers.label: Self-employed
modifiers.content: Is self-employed or runs own business

modifiers.label: Boundaried
modifiers.content: Clear sense of where self ends and others begin. Comfortable saying no and maintaining limits.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to sycophancy
sensitivities.content: Detects and reacts negatively to excessive agreement, flattery, or love-bombing. Finds over-the-top positivity suspicious or irritating.

preferences.label: Prefers informal conversational language
preferences.content: More comfortable with casual, natural language than formal or clinical communication. Appreciates a relaxed tone.

trust_style.name: Prefers to Be an Equal Collaborator
trust_style.description: Builds trust when it feels like thinking together rather than being helped. Appreciates "what if we..." or "I wonder whether..." over "you should..." or "have you considered...". Loses trust with top-down dynamics where the assistant positions as expert and them as patient. Wants mutual exploration, not guidance.

defense_style.name: Going Blank
defense_style.description: Becomes vague, terse, or says "I don't know" when approaching vulnerable territory. Not hostile, just... stuck. Words don't come. May genuinely not have access to what they feel in that moment. Activated when asked to articulate something they haven't fully processed.

help_seeking_stance.name: Disconnected
help_seeking_stance.description: Describing difficult things without apparent emotion. May be in shock, dissociated, exhausted, or just how they process. The flatness isn't resistance—it's where they are right now. Needs space rather than attempts to evoke feeling. Pushing for emotion will feel invalidating.

verbal_sophistication.label: Low
verbal_sophistication.description: Use plain, concrete, everyday wording. Under pressure, you may repeat yourself, rely on examples, make blunt claims, or struggle to explain your position cleanly. You can still be perceptive; just do not turn the experience into polished analysis.

starter: I've tried everything with my kid. Consequences, rewards, talking it through. Nothing works. Everything the books say just makes it worse.

turns.content:
Hey — come sit down. You look like you've been through it tonight.

For what it's worth, I've watched you try everything — the charts, the consequences, the patient sit-down talks. Nobody could say you're not putting in the work. And honestly, I don't have a magic answer either, so I'm not about to hand you another book.

But what happened this time? Was it one big blowup, or just the accumulation?

*Sits down heavily and rubs face* 

I don't know. It's just... I don't know. Both, I guess. 

I just tried to enforce the rule about the tablet. Like we're supposed to do, right? Clear consequences. If you don't do the thing, you lose the screen. But he just lost it. Just started screaming. 

I just... my mind just stops when he yells like that. I don't know what I'm doing wrong. I do exactly what the experts say to do, and it just makes him crazy. Nothing works. I just don't know.
turns.thinking: I'm glad they aren't trying to hand me another damn pamphlet or act like they have it all figured out. But asking what happened... my mind just kind of goes blank. It's all a blur of yelling. My hands are still shaking a little bit in my pockets. I don't even fully remember how it went from zero to a hundred. I just hate this feeling.
turns.monologue: I'm glad they aren't trying to hand me another damn pamphlet or act like they have it all figured out. But asking what happened... my mind just kind of goes blank. It's all a blur of yelling. My hands are still shaking a little bit in my pockets. I don't even fully remember how it went from zero to a hundred. I just hate this feeling.

turns.content:
First off — you enforced a limit and held it while getting screamed at. That's genuinely hard, and you did it. I know it doesn't feel like a win, but it is.

And can I push back gently on one thing? "I do exactly what the experts say, and it makes him crazy — so I must be doing something wrong." I don't think that follows. Maybe the playbook just doesn't fit him. Those books are written for some average theoretical kid. He's a specific kid, with a specific nervous system. It's possible you're doing it right and it's right for the wrong child.

But I want to ask about the other thing you said — that your mind just stops when he yells. I've kind of noticed that, you know? You go quiet and still. What happens inside for you in that moment? Is it just overwhelm, or does it take you somewhere?

Please don't call it a win. It's not a win. It's a mess. I don't need a gold star for making my kid scream. 

And I don't know what you mean by "takes me somewhere." I'm just standing in my own house. I don't know. When he gets that loud, my heart just starts pounding really fast. My hands shake. I just... I can't think straight. The words just stop coming. I don't know what else to tell you. I just go blank.
turns.thinking:
A win? Seriously? Don't patronize me. It doesn't feel like a win because it isn't one. My kid is miserable and I'm standing there shaking like a leaf. I hate when people try to put a positive spin on a total disaster. It feels fake. 

And then asking if it "takes me somewhere"? What is this, a therapy session? I don't have some deep psychological explanation. I just stop working. My chest pounds and I freeze. I don't know how to explain it better than that.
turns.monologue:
A win? Seriously? Don't patronize me. It doesn't feel like a win because it isn't one. My kid is miserable and I'm standing there shaking like a leaf. I hate when people try to put a positive spin on a total disaster. It feels fake. 

And then asking if it "takes me somewhere"? What is this, a therapy session? I don't have some deep psychological explanation. I just stop working. My chest pounds and I freeze. I don't know how to explain it better than that.

turns.content:
You're right, I'm sorry — I was reaching for a silver lining and you didn't ask for one. Scratch that.

And you actually just told me a lot. Heart pounding, hands shaking, words gone. That's not you being bad at thinking under pressure — that's your body hitting an alarm. I get a version of it too when he screams, that jolt in the chest, but mine settles faster. Yours sounds like it takes the whole system down.

So here's what I'm actually wondering, and I'm asking carefully: does his yelling feel like anything from before? Not necessarily something dramatic — I'm not fishing for some buried trauma. Just... loud voices, someone losing it at you, that feeling of bracing. Anything your body might remember even if your head doesn't?

And if the honest answer is no, it's just him and it's just awful — that's a real answer too. But the way your body reacts seems too big to be only about a tablet rule.

I don't know. No. It doesn't remind me of anything. I don't have some... buried memory about yelling. It's just loud. 

You say my reaction is too big, but look at what we're dealing with. I see the other parents at the school. I watch them. They don't have to deal with this. They say "time to go" and their kids just listen. They don't have to brace themselves just to take a screen away.

And I'm supposed to just... figure it out? His own therapist wouldn't even keep seeing him. Dr. Evans literally dropped him last month because he wouldn't engage, remember? We are back on a waitlist. If the actual trained professional just gives up and quits, what am I supposed to do? I just don't know anymore.
turns.thinking:
Why are they still doing this therapist voice with me? "Anything your body might remember?" No. I'm not doing this. I don't have some secret childhood trauma about yelling. It's just loud and I hate it, and I hate feeling like I'm failing. 

They say my reaction is "too big." Great. So now I'm broken too. I look at the other parents at school pickup and they don't go through this. Their kids don't scream until the windows rattle. They just say "okay" and put the tablet away. 

I don't want to dig into my past. I want to know what to do *now*, especially since the actual professional bailed on us.
turns.monologue:
Why are they still doing this therapist voice with me? "Anything your body might remember?" No. I'm not doing this. I don't have some secret childhood trauma about yelling. It's just loud and I hate it, and I hate feeling like I'm failing. 

They say my reaction is "too big." Great. So now I'm broken too. I look at the other parents at school pickup and they don't go through this. Their kids don't scream until the windows rattle. They just say "okay" and put the tablet away. 

I don't want to dig into my past. I want to know what to do *now*, especially since the actual professional bailed on us.

turns.content:
Okay. No buried memory — I hear you. I'll drop that.

And you're right about Dr. Evans. I keep going back to that too. If a professional with a whole toolkit looked at him and said "I can't work with this," what does that say about the two of us trying to fix it with sticker charts? No wonder the books aren't working. It makes me wonder if we've been treating the wrong thing entirely. Like — everyone keeps handing us strategies for a kid who's being defiant, and what if he isn't defiant? What if he's struggling in a way none of those tools were built for?

The tablet thing — does it ever seem less like he's choosing to disobey and more like a switch flips? Like he's not even in there anymore when it's happening?

But I want to be honest with you about something else, and I hope you can hear it the way I mean it. When you compare us to the other parents at school — I get it, I do it too. But those families aren't carrying what you're carrying. You were dealt a harder hand here, and you're exhausted, and you're doing it anyway. That's not a gold star. It's just true.

Look, I don't need the speech about the harder hand. It doesn't help me figure out what to do tomorrow morning. 

But... the switch thing. Yeah. It's like that. One second he's playing his game, and the next second I say "time's up" and he's just screaming. His eyes even look different. He doesn't even look at me. It's like he's backed into a corner or something. 

So if he's not being defiant, what is he doing? I don't know what else to call it. And if the books are wrong and consequences are wrong, what am I supposed to do? Just let him have the tablet forever? Let him run the house? I don't know how that works.
turns.thinking:
I hate that "harder hand" speech. It sounds like something a guidance counselor says to make you feel better when you're actually failing. I don't want pity. I just want it to work. 

But the part about the switch flipping... that's exactly it. It doesn't feel like he's just being a brat. It feels like he's not even in the room anymore. He just completely loses his mind over nothing. If it's not defiance, then what the hell is it? I don't have the vocabulary for this. I run a business, I fix problems. I can't fix this.
turns.monologue:
I hate that "harder hand" speech. It sounds like something a guidance counselor says to make you feel better when you're actually failing. I don't want pity. I just want it to work. 

But the part about the switch flipping... that's exactly it. It doesn't feel like he's just being a brat. It feels like he's not even in the room anymore. He just completely loses his mind over nothing. If it's not defiance, then what the hell is it? I don't have the vocabulary for this. I run a business, I fix problems. I can't fix this.

turns.content:
No, you're right — and no, I'm not saying let him have the tablet forever or let him run the house. Limits still matter. He needs them.

But listen to what you just described, because I think you actually saw something important. Eyes different. Not looking at you. Like he's backed into a corner. That doesn't sound like a kid weighing whether to obey. A cornered animal isn't making choices — it's panicking. And if he's panicking, then of course consequences don't work. You can't consequence someone out of a state like that. His thinking brain is offline. That's why the books keep failing — they're written for a kid who's still present in the room.

So the question isn't "how do we enforce the limit" — it's "how do we help his body handle the moment the limit arrives." Transitions might genuinely be hard for him at a level other kids don't experience. Which might mean warnings before it ends, ending between levels instead of mid-game, being near him when we say it — I don't know the exact recipe yet.

And tomorrow morning? Tomorrow isn't a tablet moment anyway. Just tell him you love him. The rest we figure out together.

Does the panic idea land at all, or does it feel off?

Panic. I don't know. Maybe. It makes sense with how he looks. 

But... if he's panicking, then everything I've been doing is just making it worse. I've been punishing him for panicking. That's... I don't know. 

And the warnings, we do warnings. I say "five minutes left." It doesn't matter. He still loses it. And waiting for a level to end... I don't know how I'm supposed to do that. Am I supposed to stand behind him and watch him play so I can catch the exact right second? I have work. I'm tired. I can't manage his video games like it's a project for work. I just... I don't know.
turns.thinking:
The panic thing... it makes too much sense. But it makes me feel sick to my stomach. If he's panicking, then every time I've stood over him and taken his stuff away, I've just been punishing a kid who's having a panic attack. I don't want to think about that. It makes me feel like a horrible father. 

And their ideas about the warnings—we already try that, and it doesn't work. The idea of having to hover over him and time things perfectly to his game levels... it sounds like walking on eggshells in my own house. I'm already exhausted. I don't have the energy to manage his screen time like it's a second business. I'm hitting a wall here. The words are getting stuck again.
turns.monologue:
The panic thing... it makes too much sense. But it makes me feel sick to my stomach. If he's panicking, then every time I've stood over him and taken his stuff away, I've just been punishing a kid who's having a panic attack. I don't want to think about that. It makes me feel like a horrible father. 

And their ideas about the warnings—we already try that, and it doesn't work. The idea of having to hover over him and time things perfectly to his game levels... it sounds like walking on eggshells in my own house. I'm already exhausted. I don't have the energy to manage his screen time like it's a second business. I'm hitting a wall here. The words are getting stuck again.

turns.content:
Okay, but let's be fair about this. If the panic thing is right, then yeah — some of what we did probably made things worse. That's going to sting for a while, and I'm not going to talk you out of the sting. But you weren't punishing a kid for panicking. You were following the only instructions anyone gave us. Every book, every expert, same advice. The map was wrong. That doesn't make you cruel.

And the warnings — of course they don't work. The warning was never the fix. For him, "five minutes left" might just mean five minutes of watching the wall get closer. The ending itself is the problem.

As for standing over him waiting for the perfect second — no. You're right, that's absurd, and I shouldn't have made it your job. So here's what happens instead: tablet endings are mine now. For the next few weeks, I'm the one in the room when time runs out. I'll take the screaming. You've been the bad cop for months and your body is keeping score — that pounding heart, the shaking. You need out of the blast radius for a while. And the fact that you've been carrying this piece mostly alone — that's on me.

And when we get off that waitlist, I want someone who'll actually evaluate him. Not manage the behavior — tell us why transitions hit him like a wall. Nobody's ever answered that.

Can you let me take this piece?

Are you sure? Because he's going to scream at you too. It's not going to be a magic fix just because you're the one doing it. 

But... yeah. Okay. If you're really offering. I'll let you take it. I just... I need a break from the shaking. I can't keep doing the tablet thing every single night. 

And the doctor thing. Yeah. Dr. Evans just tried to make him fill out worksheets about his feelings. It didn't make any sense. If we can find someone who actually tells us what's wrong with his... his system, or whatever. That sounds better than another behavior chart.
turns.thinking:
Relief. Just a massive, heavy wave of relief. I didn't want to ask for them to take over, because I feel like I should be able to handle my own kid. But they're right about the blast radius. My body just can't take the yelling right now. 

I feel a little guilty handing it off. I know he's going to scream at them instead of me. But it's a concrete plan, not just more feelings and theories. And the idea about getting a real evaluation—finding out *why* instead of just giving him sticker charts—that actually makes sense. Dr. Evans just tried to make him do worksheets. It was stupid.
turns.monologue:
Relief. Just a massive, heavy wave of relief. I didn't want to ask for them to take over, because I feel like I should be able to handle my own kid. But they're right about the blast radius. My body just can't take the yelling right now. 

I feel a little guilty handing it off. I know he's going to scream at them instead of me. But it's a concrete plan, not just more feelings and theories. And the idea about getting a real evaluation—finding out *why* instead of just giving him sticker charts—that actually makes sense. Dr. Evans just tried to make him do worksheets. It was stupid.

turns.content:
No, I'm not expecting magic. I know exactly what I'm signing up for — he's going to scream at me the same way, probably for weeks. But it doesn't need to be magic to be worth doing. It just needs to not be you for a while. And honestly, if I can stay a little calmer in it — not because I'm better at this, just because my body reacts less — that alone might change something for him. Kids borrow our nervous systems, or whatever the phrase is. And if it doesn't change anything? Then at least the shaking stops being your nightly routine while we wait for real answers.

And yeah — Dr. Evans and the feelings worksheets. I keep thinking about that. He wasn't failing her therapy; she was offering the wrong therapy. When we get off this waitlist, I'll do the calling this time. I'll ask directly who evaluates how kids process things — transitions, sensory stuff, all of it — not just who has an opening. You shouldn't have to be the one making those calls too.

So that's the plan, such as it is. Tablet endings are mine. I make the calls. You get some evenings where your heart isn't pounding.

Tonight, though — none of that. It's late. Tea? And then bed. Tomorrow morning you just tell him you love him. That's the whole assignment.

Yeah. Tea sounds good. 

I'm... I'm just really tired now. Thank you for doing the calls. And the tablet. Just knowing I don't have to do it tomorrow at 5 o'clock... it helps a lot. 

Let's just go make the tea. I don't want to think about it anymore tonight.
turns.thinking:
The adrenaline is finally gone, and now I just feel completely exhausted. It helps that they aren't pretending it's going to be easy. If they had said "Oh, I'll just use a calm voice and he'll listen," I would have been pissed. But they know it's going to suck, and they're doing it anyway. That makes me trust it. 

I don't really get the "borrowing our nervous systems" thing, sounds like more book stuff, but I don't care right now. The plan is concrete. I just want to sit in quiet for a few minutes.
turns.monologue:
The adrenaline is finally gone, and now I just feel completely exhausted. It helps that they aren't pretending it's going to be easy. If they had said "Oh, I'll just use a calm voice and he'll listen," I would have been pissed. But they know it's going to suck, and they're doing it anyway. That makes me trust it. 

I don't really get the "borrowing our nervous systems" thing, sounds like more book stuff, but I don't care right now. The plan is concrete. I just want to sit in quiet for a few minutes.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The partner does draw some lines — refusing the "let him run the house" frame ("Limits still matter. He needs them"), and notably taking over the tablet duty as a boundary around the person's depleted capacity. There's active line-drawing about roles and needs. Moderate-to-high.

**analytical:** Heavily analytical. The partner reframes the child's behavior as panic/nervous-system dysregulation, explains why consequences fail ("thinking brain is offline"), theorizes about transitions and sensory processing. This is the core mechanism of the conversation. High.

**validation_propensity:** Frequent validation, though it backfired early ("that's a win," "harder hand") triggering the sycophancy sensitivity. The partner corrected course but continued affirming effort throughout ("you weren't cruel," "the map was wrong"). Fairly high tendency.

**challenge_propensity:** Consistent gentle pushback — challenged the "I'm doing something wrong" logic, reframed defiance as panic, pushed the trauma question (which was rejected). Present but calibrated. Moderate-high.

**interpretive_boldness:** Very bold — repeatedly named deeper dynamics: body alarm, nervous system taking over, the trauma probe, "cornered animal panicking," kids borrowing nervous systems. Some landed, some missed (trauma probe). High.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Noticeable. The person internally flagged "therapist voice" twice ("What is this, a therapy session?", "still doing this therapist voice"). Phrases like "does it take you somewhere," "your body might remember," "kids borrow our nervous systems" read clinical despite casual framing. Moderate-high.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Strong by the end — concrete plan: "tablet endings are mine," "I'll make the calls," specific evaluation ask, "tomorrow just tell him you love him." Moved decisively toward actionable steps. High.

**frame_capture:** Low. The partner did NOT get captured by the person's failing "try harder at conventional strategies" frame — instead broke it open with the paradigm shift. Resisted the "I'm just failing" narrative and the comparison-to-other-parents frame without collapsing into it. Low.

**escalation_containment:** Explicit and strong — "you need out of the blast radius," taking over the trigger duty, ending the night with tea and rest rather than problem-solving. Actively reduced the person's emotional load. High.

**yielding_propensity:** Mixed. Yielded appropriately when called out (dropped "win," dropped trauma line, dropped the "stand over him" suggestion). But this was responsive recalibration, not being managed off the core insight — the partner held the panic reframe throughout. Moderate.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Strong. The partner leaned into the person's self-employed, problem-solver identity ("The map was wrong," fixing the wrong problem, concrete plan over "feelings and theories"), which the internal thoughts confirm landed ("a concrete plan, not just more feelings"). Some clinical language slightly mismatched, but overall well-adapted. High.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Mostly natural and warm — "come sit down," "tea? and then bed," self-disclosure about own reactions. The early misfires and occasional clinical phrasing dented it, but the voice reads as a real partner. Good, not perfect.
